title: Secondary Analysis of Electronic Health Records
authors: Charlton P, Ghassemi M, Johnson AE, Komorowski M, Marshall D, Neumann T, Paik K, Pollard T, Raffa J, Salciccioli J
abstract: This is the first book published that describes the process of curating, exploring and analyzing messy electronic health record data. Like a cookbook, it comes with SQL and R codes and queries to demonstrate each step using the publicly available Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care (MIMIC), a database of patients admitted to the intensive care units of the Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center. The open-access book has been downloaded close to 1.7 million times since its publication in 2016, and has been translated to Spanish, Mandarin, Portuguese and Korean.

title: MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database.
authors: Johnson AEW, Pollard TJ, Shen L, Lehman L, Feng M, Ghassemi M, Moody B, Szolovits P, Celi LA, Mark RG.
abstract: This paper is a description of the MIMIC-III dataset. It has been cited more than 5000 times since its publication in 2016. Leo Celi, lead ICU clinician worked with the engineers at MIT who design and implement a data schema that is intuitive and machine learning and clinical research-friendly.

title: Assessment of Racial and Ethnic Differences in Oxygen Supplementation Among Patients in the Intensive Care Unit
authors: Gottlieb ER, Ziegler J, Morley K, Rush B, Celi LA
abstract: The manuscript reported that Asian, Black, and Hispanic patients received less supplemental oxygen than White patients after adjusting for clinical confounding. The gap is fully explained by the pulse oximeter performance. With an Altmetric of 2161, the paper is one of the most discussed (top 5%) research manuscript in mainstream and social media ever. It was reported by 333 news stories from 250 outlets, and was featured prominently during public online dialogues that were organized by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to address the differential performance of pulse oximeters based on the skin tone.

title: Discrepancies Between Pulse Oximetry and Arterial Oxygen Saturation Measurements by Race and Ethnicity and Association With Organ Dysfunction and Mortality.
authors: Wong AI, Charpignon M, Kim H, Josef C, de Hond AAH, Fojas JJ, Tabaie A, Liu X, Mireles-Cabodevila E, Carvalho L, Kamaleswaran R, Madushani RWMA, Adhikari L, Holder AL, Steyerberg EW, Buchman TG, Lough ME, Celi LA.
abstract: In this study, there was greater variability in oxygen saturation levels for a given Spo2 level in patients who self-identified as Black, followed by Hispanic, Asian, and White. Patients with and without hidden hypoxemia were demographically and clinically similar at baseline ABG measurement by SOFA scores, but those with hidden hypoxemia subsequently experienced higher organ dysfunction scores and higher in-hospital mortality. Similar to the prior manuscript, and with an Altmetric of 1039, the paper is one of the most discussed (top 5%) research manuscript in mainstream and social media ever. It was reported by 135 news stories from 129 outlets, and was also featured prominently during public online dialogues that were organized by the FDA to address the differential performance of pulse oximeters based on the skin tone.

title: The Artificial Intelligence Clinician learns optimal treatment strategies for sepsis in intensive care
authors: Komorowski M, Celi LA, Badawi O, Gordon AC, Faisal AA.
abstract: This is the first paper published that employed reinforcement learning on electronic health record data. This article has been cited more than 800 times according to Google Scholar, and is in the 99th percentile of the 354,712 articles tracked by Altmetric of a similar age in all journals. It is ranked 4th of the 113 tracked articles of a similar age in Nature Medicine. Our expertise with the MIMIC database was used to train the model.

title: AI recognition of patient race in medical imaging: a modelling study
authors: Gichoya JW, Banerjee I, Bhimireddy AR, Burns JL, Celi LA, Chen LC, Correa R, Dullerud N, Ghassemi M, Huang SC, Kuo PC, Lungren MP, Palmer LJ, Price BJ, Purkayastha S, Pyrros AT, Oakden-Rayner L, Okechukwu C, Seyyed-Kalantari L, Trivedi H, Wang R, Zaiman Z, Zhang H
abstract: In this paper, we demonstrated that computers can learn the race-ethnicity from medical images in the absence of any clinical data. What was most perplexing was that we (and others who read the paper) couldn’t work out what the computers were learning to formulate these identifications. They didn’t rely on obvious confounders such as disease distribution, nor did they rely on specific anatomic regions such as bones and soft tissue, or specific portions of the image spectrum. When a demographic attribute such as race-ethnicity is learnable even when deliberately removed from a dataset, then it is not far-fetched to think that computers will also pick-up provider bias and subjectivity in decision-making.

title: Guidelines for reinforcement learning in healthcare
authors: Gottesman O, Johansson F, Komorowski M, Faisal A, Sontag D, Doshi-Velez F, Celi LA
abstract: This paper describes the best practices in the application of reinforcement learning on healthcare data. It has been cited 352 times so far and is in the 98th percentile of the 445,021 articles tracked by Altmetric of a similar age in all journals.

title: Developing and Validating Multi-Modal Models for Mortality Prediction in COVID-19 Patients: a Multi-center Retrospective Study
authors: Wu JT*, de la Hoz MÁA*, Kuo PC*, Paguio JA, Yao JS, Dee EC, Yeung W, Jurado J, Moulick A, Milazzo C, Peinado P, Villares P, Cubillo A, Varona JF, Lee HC, Estirado A, Castellano JM, Celi LA
abstract: We have published numerous papers in machine learning not just in critical care medicine but across different specialties such as ophthalmology, radiology, surgery, nursing, bioethics, among others. We shared a blueprint for multi-modal modeling that included medical images as feature and published it in a top-tier medical imaging journal.

title: A new tool for evaluating health equity in academic journals; the Diversity Factor.
authors: Gallifant J, Zhang J, Whebell S, Quion J, Escobar B, Gichoya J, Herrera K, Jina R, Chidambaram S, Mehndiratta A, Kimera R, Marcelo A, Fernandez-Marcelo PG, Osorio JS, Villanueva C, Nazer L, Dankwa-Mullan I, Celi LA.
abstract: We introduce the concept of a diversity factor for journals. Given that journals play a huge role in the creation and dissemination of knowledge, it’s time to downgrade the importance of the impact factor, a measure of how often papers published in a journal are cited. The impact factor feeds into the market of lemons where the rich, who controls the knowledge economy, get richer. We know that the lack of diversity among those who create knowledge contributes to a research agenda that fuels health disparities. Isn’t it time to make journals accountable for enabling this lack of diversity?

title: The myth of generalisability in clinical research and machine learning in health care.
authors: Futoma J, Simons M, Panch T, Doshi-Velez F, Celi LA.
abstract: This paper, which has been cited 212 times so far, addresses the second most critical issue in artificial intelligence (after data bias). The pursuit of model generalizability is misguided given that the accuracy of every model is bound by both space and time. AI in healthcare cannot be patented because it needs to be finetuned before deploying outside of the institutions where the training data was obtained. It also requires continuous monitoring and recalibration because of temporal data drift.

title: Reform Response - Tying Equity To Reimbursements
authors: Robin L. Pierce, Jack Gallifant, Leo Anthony Celi
abstract: In December 2022, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services proposed a bold initiative to tie reimbursements to health equity. The proposal puts forth a set of new and revised provisions targeting specific pathways that are set to enhance health equity. While it takes a much-needed step toward actualizing a commitment to health equity, more than good intentions are required to ensure its success. Several key decisions are yet to be made, which could derail this promising initiative’s ability to make meaningful inroads toward health equity. In this paper, we point to critical considerations that need to be addressed before the intiative takes effect, including modifications to the roles of health care providiers, organizations, and policy makers.

title: Role of artificial intelligence in pharmacy practice - A narrative review
authors: Adrian Wong, Erin Wentz, Nicholas Palisano, Manar Dirani, Pansy Elsamadisi, Farah Qashou, Leo Celi, Omar Badawi, Lama Nazer
abstract: With the growth of electronic health records, digital health platforms, and social media, there is an abundance of data that can be analyzed using AI to generate insights that can help pharmacy practice make informed decisions and provide personalized care to their patients. This can ultimately improve patient outcomes and reduce healthcare costs. This narrative review evaluates the potential roles that AI technology with three guided questions to determine how AI may improve patient outcomes.

title: Delivering on NIH data sharing requirements - avoiding Open Data in Appearance Only
authors: Hope Watson, Jack Gallifant, Yuan Lai, Alexander P Radunsky, Cleva Villanueva, Nicole Martinez, Judy Gichoya, Uyen Kim Huynh, Leo Anthony Celi
abstract: In January, the National Institutes of Health (NIH) implemented a Data Management and Sharing Policy aiming to leverage data collected during NIH-funded research. The COVID-19 pandemic illustrated that this practice is equally vital for augmenting patient research. In addition, data sharing acts as a necessary safeguard against the introduction of analytical biases. While the pandemic provided an opportunity to curtail critical research issues such as reproducibility and validity through data sharing, this did not materialise in practice and became an example of ‘Open Data in Appearance Only’ (ODIAO). Here, we define ODIAO as the intent of data sharing without the occurrence of actual data sharing (eg, material or digital data transfers).

title: A guide to sharing open healthcare data under the General Data Protection Regulation
authors: Jip W. T. M. de Kok, Miguel A. Armengol de la Hoz, Ymke de Jong, Veronique Brokke, Paul W. G. Elbers, Patrick Thoral, Alejandro Castillejo, Tomas Trenor, Jose M. Castellano, Alberto E. Bronchalo, Tobias M. Merz, Martin Faltys, Iwan C. C. van der Horst, Minnan Xu, Leo Anthony Celi, Bas C. T. van Bussel, Xavier Borrat
abstract: The data bias within health records is too complex for a siloed small group of investigators to understand. Any health AI initiative without data sharing at its core is doomed to legitimize and even scale systemic inequities.

title: A guide to sharing open healthcare data under the General Data Protection Regulation
authors: Marie-Laure Charpignon, Joseph Byers, Stephanie Cabral, Leo Anthony Celi, Chrystinne Fernandes, Jack Gallifant, Mary E. Lough, Donald Mlombwa, Lama Moukheiber, Bradley Ashley Ong, Anupol Panitchote, Wasswa William, An-Kwok Ian Wong, Lama Nazer
abstract: Critical care data reflect the most physiologically unstable patients in a hospital. These patients are heavily monitored and may undergo complex treatment regimens to manage multiple organ failure. The intensive care unit (ICU) demands a rich volume of information continuously collected from the patient so that physicians can decide on immediate interventions and clinical responses to be measured.

title: Fairness metrics for health AI - We have a long way to go
authors: Amarachi B. Mbakwe, Ismini Lourentzou, Leo Anthony Celi, Joy T Wu
abstract: A critical step towards addressing AI model bias and subgroup disparities is the establishment of common principles, guidelines, and standards that model developers adhere to. These standards would need to emphasize the importance of fairness and trasparency in AI systems design and deployment.

title: ChatGPT passing USMLE shines a spotlight on the flaws of medical eduction
authors: Amarachi Mbakwe, Ismini Lourentzou, Leo Anthony Celi, Oren Mechanic, Alon Dagan
abstract: The goal of the USMLE exam is to assess “a physician's ability to apply knowledge, concepts, and principles, and to demonstrate fundamental patient-centered skills that are important in health and disease that constitute the basis of safe and effective patient care”. While achieving these goals does require some rote memorization, we are increasingly realizing that the ability to regurgitate mechanistic models of health and disease, as in what often takes place during rounds, may be less important in this age of information being rapidly accessible at your fingertips.

title: An extension to the FDA approval process is needed to achieve AI equity
authors: Alessandro Hammond, Bhav Jain, Leo Anthony Celi, Fatima Cody Stanford
abstract: To achieve health equity, the FDA should mandate that manufacturers test their medical devices and software on diverse patient populations. In addition, companies should provide information on the composition of patients who participated in the design and calibration of the device or software.

title: Titration of Ventilator Settings to Target Driving Pressure and Mechanical Power
authors: Elias Baedorf Kassis, Stephanie Hu, MingYu Lu, Alistair Johnson, Somnath Bose, Maximilian S Schaefer, Daniel Talmor, Li-Wei H Lehman, Zach Shahn
abstract: Driving pressure and mechanical power may be important mediators of lung injury in ARDS; however, there is little evidence for strategies directed at reducing these parameters. We applied predictive modeling to estimate the effects of modifying ventilator parameters on driving pressure and mechanical power
